Article.ArticleTitle: Discovery and preclinical evaluation of monoclonal antibodies and bispecific engagers targeting the NKG2A inhibitory receptor.

Abstract.AbstractText: NK and T cells are key effectors that eliminate cancer cells, but upregulation of the inhibitory receptor NKG2A on these cells attenuates antitumor immune responses. To counteract NKG2A inhibitory signaling, we identified two specific fully human monoclonal anti-NKG2A antibodies that block HLA-E ligand binding. These antibodies activated NK cells and enhanced antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity of tumor-targeting IgG1s both in vitro and in vivo. Bispecific engagers (BiNKs), generated by fusing NKG2A antibodies with tumor targeting binders, promoted immune synapse formation and directed cytotoxicity of NK and CD8+ T cells toward cancer cells. In a human PBMC-engrafted NSG mouse xenograft lung cancer model, an anti-HER2 × anti-NKG2A BiNK markedly inhibited tumor growth as a monotherapy or in combination with pertuzumab. Cell depletion studies revealed that the BiNK enhanced antitumor activity of both NK and T cells. NKG2A blockade with potent and specific, fully human antibodies and BiNKs show promise for further development as cancer immunotherapeutics.

Chemical.NameOfSubstance: NK Cell Lectin-Like Receptor Subfamily C

MeshHeading.DescriptorName: NK Cell Lectin-Like Receptor Subfamily C

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: J.W.M. is a consultant to Gilead Sciences and owns shares in Infectious Disease Connect, Galapagos NV, and MingMed, unrelated to the current work. D.S.B., Y.J.K., J.W.M., and D.S.D. are inventors on a patent application (US63/289,495) to NKG2A antibodies in this manuscript filed by University of Pittsburgh. All other authors declare that they have no competing interests.

Article.ArticleTitle: Evaluation of the cross-immunogenicity and protective efficacy of the multi-epitope antigen Tzi from Glasserella parasuis in mouse model.

Abstract.AbstractText: Glässer's disease is an important swine respiratory infectious disease caused by Glaesserella parasuis (G. parasuis), which leads to considerable economic losses in the swine industry. At present, the primary method for preventing Glässer's disease is the use of inactivated vaccines. However, inactivated vaccines provide limited protection against infections caused by heterologous serotypes of G. parasuis, thereby limiting their overall efficacy. To develop a G. parasuis vaccine with enhanced cross-protective efficacy, this study utilized bioinformatics approaches to predict and analyze B-cell (B lymphocyte), cytotoxic T lymphocyte (CTL), and helper T lymphocyte (Th) epitopes of G. parasuis antigenic proteins, including The Pili Subunit A (PilA), Iron donor protein CyaY protein, ABC transporter substrate-binding protein A (HbpA), and Cytolethal distending toxin subunit B (CtdB). These epitopes were connected via linkers and cleavage sites, and an intrastrand adjuvant was added to construct the multi-epitope antigen Tzi. The results of molecular docking and immune system simulation indicated that the multi-epitope protein Tzi has the potential to activate immune responses. Western blotting results showed that Tzi could interact with mouse antisera against 6 serotypes (4, 5, 10, 12, 14 and non-typeable) of G. parasuis. In vitro antibacterial assays demonstrated that mouse antisera against Tzi exhibited bactericidal activity against six serotypes of G. parasuis. Serum levels of antibodies, Interleukin-4 (IL-4), and Interferon gamma (IFN-γ) were significantly higher in Tzi-immunized mice than in the adjuvant group. Similarly, the concentrations of Interleukin-2 (IL-2), IL-4, Interleukin-12 subunit alpha (IL-12A), and IFN-γ were markedly higher in both splenic and pulmonary tissues compared to the adjuvant group. Mice immunized with Tzi were challenged with serotypes 4, 5, and 12 of G. parasuis, and the survival rates were 50 %, 62.5 %, and 87.5 %, respectively. Moreover, compared with the adjuvant group, the clinical symptoms and lung tissue lesions in Tzi-immunized mice were significantly alleviated. Overall, the multi-epitope protein Tzi exhibited cross-immunogenicity, effectively induced both humoral and cellular immune responses in mice, and offered robust cross-protection against G. parasuis serotypes 4, 5, and 12, thereby indicating its potential as a promising candidate for a subunit vaccine against G. parasuis.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: Copyright © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
